# Relational Access Control Rule in PocketBase

## Background

Configure API rules in PocketBase to enforce relational access control. You need to create collections for `projects` and `tasks`, and restrict access to tasks based on project membership.

## Requirements

* Initialize a PocketBase standalone application.
* Create a `projects` collection with fields: `name` (text) and `members` (relation to `users` collection, multiple).
* Create a `tasks` collection with fields: `title` (text) and `project` (relation to `projects` collection, single).
* Configure the `listRule` and `viewRule` on the `tasks` collection so that users can only list and view tasks if they are assigned to the parent `project` via the `members` relational field.
* The `listRule` and `viewRule` for `projects` can be set to allow members to view them, or left open for testing, but the primary requirement is the `tasks` collection rules.
* Implement this using JS migrations in the `pb_migrations` directory so that it runs automatically when the server starts.

## Implementation Hints

* Download the PocketBase binary (v0.31.0 for linux amd64) and extract it to the project path.
* Create a JS migration file in the `pb_migrations` directory to programmatically create the collections and set the API rules.
* The API rule for relational checks uses dot notation (e.g., `@request.auth.id ?= project.members`).
* Ensure the PocketBase server binds to `0.0.0.0:8090`.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/myproject
* Start command: ./pocketbase serve --http="0.0.0.0:8090"
* Port: 8090
* Collections:
  * `projects` collection exists with `name` (text) and `members` (relation to `users`).
  * `tasks` collection exists with `title` (text) and `project` (relation to `projects`).
* API Rules:
  * A user authenticated via the REST API can list and view a task ONLY IF they are in the `members` array of the task's parent project.
  * A user cannot list or view tasks belonging to projects they are not members of.

# pocketbase/CHANGELOG.md at master - GitHub
created: 2022-07-05, last updated: 2026-04-16, [Read more](https://github.com/pocketbase/pocketbase/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md)

PocketBase v0.23.0 is a major refactor of the internals with the overall goal of making PocketBase an easier to use Go framework.
...
- New hooks allowing better control over the execution chain and error handling (*including wrapping an entire hook chain in a single DB transaction*).
- Various `Record` model improvements (*support for get/set modifiers, simplfied file upload by treating the file(s) as regular field value like `record.Set("document", file)`, etc.*).
- Dedicated fields structs with safer defaults to make it easier creating/updating collections programmatically.
- Option to mark field as "Hidden", disallowing regular users to read or modify it (*there is also a dedicated Record hook to hide/unhide Record fields programmatically from a single place*).
...
This behaviour has changed with v0.23+ and for consistency with the other multi-valued fields when uploading new files they will replace the old ones.
...
- ⚠️ New flatten Collection model and fields structure.
The Collection model Web APIs are mostly used by the Dashboard UI and rarely by the end users, but if you want to check all changes please refer to the Collection Go struct.
...
The error field key remains `code`:

# Collections
created: 2022-01-01, last updated: 2026-06-14, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/collections/)

**Collections** represent your application data.
Under the hood they are backed by plain SQLite tables that are generated automatically with the collection **name** and **fields** (columns).
Single entry of a collection is called **record** (a single row in the SQL table).
You can manage your **collections** from the Dashboard, with the Web APIs using the client-side SDKs (*superusers only*) or programmatically via the Go/JavaScript migrations.
...
Currently there are 3 collection types: **Base**, **View** and **Auth**.
...
**Base collection** is the default collection type and it could be used to store any application data (articles, products, posts, etc.).

### Migration Execution Order
1. **System migrations**: Core schema (e.g., `1640988000_init.go`)

- Import schema to test instance
- Verify migration generation
- Test with representative data volume

- Verify `up()` and `down()` functions

## Snapshot Migrations
`./pocketbase migrate collections` generates a complete snapshot — useful for:
- Bootstrapping a new environment
- Resetting migration history
- Reviewing full schema in one file
The generated file uses `app.importCollections(collections)` which supports two modes:
- **Default (merge/extend)**: adds new collections and fields, updates existing ones, doesn't delete anything
- **Delete missing**: `app.importCollections(collections, true)` — deletes collections/fields not in the snapshot

The `relation` fields follow the same rules as any other collection field and can be set/modified
by directly updating the field value - with a record id or array of ids, in case a multiple relation is used.
...
To prepend/append a single or multiple relation id(s) to an existing value you can use the
`+` field modifier:
...
To remove a single or multiple relation id(s) from an existing value you can use the
`-` field modifier:
...
You can also expand record relation fields directly in the returned response without making additional
requests by using the `expand` query parameter, e.g. `?expand=user,post.tags`
Only the relations that the request client can **View** (aka.
satisfies the relation collection's **View API Rule**) will be expanded.
...
PocketBase supports also `filter`, `sort` and `expand` for
**back-relations** - relations where the associated `relation` field is not in the main collection.
The following notation is used: `referenceCollection**_via_**relField` (ex.
`comments_via_post`).

# Class RelationField - PocketBase JSVM reference
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-05-03, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/jsvm/classes/RelationField.html)

RelationField defines "relation" type field for storing single or
multiple collection record references.
Requires the CollectionId option to be set.
If MaxSelect is not set or <= 1, then the field value is expected to be a single record id.
If MaxSelect is > 1, then the field value is expected to be a slice of record ids.
...
### collection Id
collectionId: string
CollectionId is the id of the related collection.
…
### id
id: string
Id is the unique stable field identifier.
It is automatically generated from the name when adding to a collection FieldsList.
...
MaxSelect indicates the max number of allowed relation records
that could be linked to the main model.
For multiple select the value must be > 1, otherwise fallbacks to single (default).
If MinSelect is set, MaxSelect must be at least >= MinSelect.
...
MinSelect indicates the min number of allowed relation records
that could be linked to the main model.
No min limit is applied if it is zero or negative value.
### name
name: string
Name (required) is the unique name of the field.
...
Required will require the field value to be non-empty.

```
**organizations** collection, for **List** and **View:**
@request.auth.id != "" && (owners ?= @request.auth.id || members ?= @request.auth.id)
which generates this SQL
...
If I change the API Rule to:
@request.auth.id != "" && (owners:each = @request.auth.id || members:each @request.auth.id)
...
Change `owners` and `members` to `owners.id` and `members.id`.
Your rule is wrong because pb is comparing the value that is stored in the table, which for "many" relations is a json array, against your id.
I recommend DBeaver and exploring how the data is stored.
The ":each" also should work (I've recently changed our code from `~` to it as it is more efficient) but you need "?" in front of the equal operators for any match (if you inspect the query you will see that the ":each" generates a SQLite json_each statement).
...
The field is a relation field, and even tho the "value" stored in that field is the FK id itself, because its a relation, it needs to be addressed as such.

It is possible that the token has expired.
Keep in mind that
```
pb.authStore.model
```
is the last fetched state of your auth record and it doesn't guarantee that it is valid or that you are authenticated.
PocketBase is stateless, aka.
there are no sessions in the traditional sense.
A request is considered authenticated only when there is a valid non-expired
```
Authorization: TOKEN
```
header with the request, which is added automatically by the SDK with each request call.
...
Are you sure that you are authenticated when sending the client request?
*If you are using the SDKs, for the test you can print
```
pb.authStore.isValid
```
before the request call.*
...
Another thing that can fail the rule is if
```
user
```
is a multiple
```
relation
```
field (aka. it can have more than one user assigned).
If that's the case either change it to single (*note that older PocketBase version doesn't update already inserted records*) or use the
```
?=
```
operator.
```

```
The 2
```
companies_via_users.users.id
```
expressions target a single id value and it cannot be 2 different things (
```
@request.auth.id

```
and
```
id

```
But if I'm understanding your collections structure I don't think you need to do that.
Your API rule could look like:
```
@request.auth.id != "" && (
id = @request.auth.id ||
public = true ||
@request.auth.companies_via_users.users.id ?= id
)

@request.auth.id != "" && (

# Class RelationField - PocketBase JSVM reference
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-05-03, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/jsvm/classes/RelationField.html)

RelationField defines "relation" type field for storing single or
multiple collection record references.
Requires the CollectionId option to be set.
If MaxSelect is not set or <= 1, then the field value is expected to be a single record id.
If MaxSelect is > 1, then the field value is expected to be a slice of record ids.
The respective zero record field value is either empty string (single) or empty string slice (multiple).
...
### is Multiple
- isMultiple(): boolean
- IsMultiple implements [MultiValuer] interface and checks whether the
current field options support multiple values.
#### Returns boolean
...
### max Select
maxSelect: number
MaxSelect indicates the max number of allowed relation records
that could be linked to the main model.
For multiple select the value must be > 1, otherwise fallbacks to single (default).
If MinSelect is set, MaxSelect must be at least >= MinSelect.
### min Select
minSelect: number
MinSelect indicates the min number of allowed relation records
that could be linked to the main model.
No min limit is applied if it is zero or negative value.

# Relation field - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-07, [Read more](https://pocketbase-pocketbase.mintlify.app/fields/relation)

Minimum number of required relation records.
Set to 0 for no minimum limit.
...
<ParamField path="maxSelect" type="int" default="1">
Maximum number of relation records that can be linked.
Set to 1 (or less) for single relation mode.
Set to > 1 for multiple relations mode.
</ParamField>
...
The relation field validates:
* **Collection exists**: Referenced collection must exist and be accessible
* **Records exist**: All referenced record IDs must exist in the related collection
* **Min select**: If set, minimum number of relations must be met
* **Max select**: Number of relations cannot exceed the maximum
* **Required**: If enabled, at least one relation must be set
...
## Single vs multiple relations
<Info>
The field behavior changes based on `maxSelect`:
* `maxSelect <= 1`: Single relation mode (value is a record ID string)
* `maxSelect > 1`: Multiple relations mode (value is an array of record IDs)
</Info>
...
## Zero value
* **Single relation**: Empty string `""`
* **Multiple relations**: Empty array `[]`

import requests
import json
import sys

BASE_URL = "http://127.0.0.1:8090"

def run_test():
    print("Starting integration test for relational access control rules...")

    # 1. Create two users
    print("\n--- Creating Users ---")
    user1_email = "user1@example.com"
    user2_email = "user2@example.com"
    password = "password12345"

    users = [user1_email, user2_email]
    user_ids = {}

    for email in users:
        # Check if user already exists or create new
        resp = requests.post(
            f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/users/records",
            json={
                "email": email,
                "password": password,
                "passwordConfirm": password
            }
        )
        if resp.status_code in [200, 201]:
            data = resp.json()
            user_ids[email] = data["id"]
            print(f"Created user {email} with ID {data['id']}")
        elif resp.status_code == 400:
            # Maybe already exists, let's try to authenticate first to get the ID
            print(f"User {email} might already exist, authenticating...")
            auth_resp = requests.post(
                f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/users/auth-with-password",
                json={"identity": email, "password": password}
            )
            if auth_resp.status_code == 200:
                data = auth_resp.json()
                user_ids[email] = data["record"]["id"]
                print(f"Authenticated existing user {email} with ID {user_ids[email]}")
            else:
                print(f"Failed to authenticate/create user {email}: {auth_resp.text}")
                sys.exit(1)
        else:
            print(f"Failed to create user {email}: {resp.status_code} - {resp.text}")
            sys.exit(1)

    # 2. Authenticate both users to get tokens
    print("\n--- Authenticating Users ---")
    tokens = {}
    for email in users:
        resp = requests.post(
            f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/users/auth-with-password",
            json={"identity": email, "password": password}
        )
        if resp.status_code == 200:
            tokens[email] = resp.json()["token"]
            print(f"Obtained token for {email}")
        else:
            print(f"Authentication failed for {email}: {resp.text}")
            sys.exit(1)

    headers_user1 = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {tokens[user1_email]}"}
    headers_user2 = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {tokens[user2_email]}"}

    # 3. Create Project A (User 1 is member) and Project B (User 2 is member)
    print("\n--- Creating Projects ---")
    # Project A
    resp_proj_a = requests.post(
        f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/projects/records",
        headers=headers_user1,
        json={
            "name": "Project A",
            "members": [user_ids[user1_email]]
        }
    )
    if resp_proj_a.status_code in [200, 201]:
        proj_a_id = resp_proj_a.json()["id"]
        print(f"Created Project A with ID {proj_a_id}")
    else:
        print(f"Failed to create Project A: {resp_proj_a.text}")
        sys.exit(1)

    # Project B
    resp_proj_b = requests.post(
        f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/projects/records",
        headers=headers_user2,
        json={
            "name": "Project B",
            "members": [user_ids[user2_email]]
        }
    )
    if resp_proj_b.status_code in [200, 201]:
        proj_b_id = resp_proj_b.json()["id"]
        print(f"Created Project B with ID {proj_b_id}")
    else:
        print(f"Failed to create Project B: {resp_proj_b.text}")
        sys.exit(1)

    # 4. Create Task A (belongs to Project A) and Task B (belongs to Project B)
    print("\n--- Creating Tasks ---")
    resp_task_a = requests.post(
        f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/tasks/records",
        headers=headers_user1,
        json={
            "title": "Task A",
            "project": proj_a_id
        }
    )
    if resp_task_a.status_code in [200, 201]:
        task_a_id = resp_task_a.json()["id"]
        print(f"Created Task A with ID {task_a_id}")
    else:
        print(f"Failed to create Task A: {resp_task_a.text}")
        sys.exit(1)

    resp_task_b = requests.post(
        f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/tasks/records",
        headers=headers_user2,
        json={
            "title": "Task B",
            "project": proj_b_id
        }
    )
    if resp_task_b.status_code in [200, 201]:
        task_b_id = resp_task_b.json()["id"]
        print(f"Created Task B with ID {task_b_id}")
    else:
        print(f"Failed to create Task B: {resp_task_b.text}")
        sys.exit(1)

    # 5. Verify User 1 access
    print("\n--- Verifying User 1 Access ---")
    # List tasks
    resp_list_1 = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/tasks/records", headers=headers_user1)
    if resp_list_1.status_code == 200:
        items = resp_list_1.json()["items"]
        titles = [item["title"] for item in items]
        print(f"User 1 listed tasks: {titles}")
        assert "Task A" in titles, "User 1 should see Task A"
        assert "Task B" not in titles, "User 1 should NOT see Task B"
        print("PASS: List rules correctly restrict User 1 to Project A tasks.")
    else:
        print(f"User 1 list failed: {resp_list_1.text}")
        sys.exit(1)

    # View Task A (should pass)
    resp_view_a_1 = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/tasks/records/{task_a_id}", headers=headers_user1)
    assert resp_view_a_1.status_code == 200, f"User 1 should be able to view Task A, got {resp_view_a_1.status_code}"
    print("PASS: User 1 can view Task A.")

    # View Task B (should fail)
    resp_view_b_1 = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/tasks/records/{task_b_id}", headers=headers_user1)
    assert resp_view_b_1.status_code in [403, 404], f"User 1 should NOT be able to view Task B, got {resp_view_b_1.status_code}"
    print("PASS: User 1 cannot view Task B.")

    # 6. Verify User 2 access
    print("\n--- Verifying User 2 Access ---")
    # List tasks
    resp_list_2 = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/tasks/records", headers=headers_user2)
    if resp_list_2.status_code == 200:
        items = resp_list_2.json()["items"]
        titles = [item["title"] for item in items]
        print(f"User 2 listed tasks: {titles}")
        assert "Task B" in titles, "User 2 should see Task B"
        assert "Task A" not in titles, "User 2 should NOT see Task A"
        print("PASS: List rules correctly restrict User 2 to Project B tasks.")
    else:
        print(f"User 2 list failed: {resp_list_2.text}")
        sys.exit(1)

    # View Task B (should pass)
    resp_view_b_2 = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/tasks/records/{task_b_id}", headers=headers_user2)
    assert resp_view_b_2.status_code == 200, f"User 2 should be able to view Task B, got {resp_view_b_2.status_code}"
    print("PASS: User 2 can view Task B.")

    # View Task A (should fail)
    resp_view_a_2 = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/collections/tasks/records/{task_a_id}", headers=headers_user2)
    assert resp_view_a_2.status_code in [403, 404], f"User 2 should NOT be able to view Task A, got {resp_view_a_2.status_code}"
    print("PASS: User 2 cannot view Task A.")

    print("\n=============================================")
    print("SUCCESS: All access control tests passed!")
    print("=============================================")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_test()

`members`refer to the raw value, which in case of a multiple

The
`members ~ "id"`operator works because it applies a
`LIKE`string matching expression against the raw serialized json array.

To target a specific relation field you can use dot-notation:

# Introduction - API rules and filters - Docs - PocketBase
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-11, [Read more](https://pocketbase.io/docs/api-rules-and-filters/)

**API Rules** are your collection access controls and data filters.
Each collection has **5 rules**, corresponding to the specific API action:
- `listRule`
- `viewRule`
- `createRule`
- `updateRule`
- `deleteRule`
...
Each rule could be set to:
- **"locked"** - aka.
`null`, which means that the action could be performed
...
- **Empty string** - anyone will be able to perform the action (superusers, authorized users and guests)
- **Non-empty string** - only users (authorized or not) that satisfy the rule filter expression will be able to perform this action
...
can define an alias by appending `:alias` suffix to the collection name.
...
- Allow only registered users:
`@request.auth.id != ""`
- Allow only registered users and return records that are either "active" or "pending":
`@request.auth.id != "" && (status = "active" || status = "pending")`
- Allow only registered users who are listed in an *allowed_users* multi-relation field value:
`@request.auth.id != "" && allowed_users.id ?= @request.auth.id`

input.result:
I have successfully configured relational access control rules in PocketBase v0.31.0 for the `projects` and `tasks` collections using a JavaScript migration.

### Implementation Details

1. **JavaScript Migration file**: Created a migration file `pb_migrations/1781532463_create_collections.js` that programmatically defines the required collections and rules on startup:
   - **`projects` collection**:
     - `name` (text, required)
     - `members` (relation to `users` collection, multiple select up to 999)
     - Rules: Allows authenticated users to view, list, create, update, and delete projects.
   - **`tasks` collection**:
     - `title` (text, required)
     - `project` (relation to `projects` collection, single select)
     - Rules:
       - `listRule` & `viewRule` are set to `@request.auth.id ?= project.members.id`
       - This ensures that a user can only list and view tasks belonging to projects of which they are a member.
       - `createRule`, `updateRule`, and `deleteRule` are set to `@request.auth.id != ""` for testing purposes.

2. **PocketBase Server**:
   - The server is running and bound to `0.0.0.0:8090` using the command `./pocketbase serve --http="0.0.0.0:8090"`.
   - The migrations automatically ran and initialized the collections on startup.

3. **Verification**:
   - Created and executed an integration test script (`test_rules.py`) in Python that successfully verified the relational access control rules:
     - Created `user1` and `user2`.
     - Created `Project A` (with `user1` as member) and `Project B` (with `user2` as member).
     - Created `Task A` (under `Project A`) and `Task B` (under `Project B`).
     - Verified that `user1` can list and view `Task A` but is restricted from listing or viewing `Task B`.
     - Verified that `user2` can list and view `Task B` but is restricted from listing or viewing `Task A`.
     - De-registered and cleaned up the test script to keep the workspace clean. All tests passed perfectly!
